articles.title: Moon phase today: What the Moon will look like on June 18
articles.description: See the Moon phase expected for June 18, 2026 as well as when the next Full Moon is expected.

articles.title: Europe’s first superconducting X-ray spectrometer boosts photon detection by 1000 times
articles.description: The new superconducting detector array at Germany's BESSY II can perform some X-ray measurements in minutes instead of hours.

articles.title: Silicon chip creates 64 DNA sequences in parallel using electric currents and water
articles.description: Harvard researchers developed a semiconductor chip that synthesizes 64 DNA sequences using electricity and water-based enzymatic chemistry.

articles.title: New ‘electronic nose’ uses 16 gas sensors to detect food spoilage and allergens
articles.description: UC Berkeley researchers built an electronic nose that uses carbon nanotube sensors and AI to detect food spoilage and identify allergens.

articles.title: Simpler, older version of Stonehenge found three miles from famous site
articles.description: The structure consisted of two posts that lined up with the solstices 5,000 years ago.

articles.title: Reddit study highlights user purchase behavior
articles.description: The research, created in partnership with WPP, examined how people on the platform gain insights into brands and make buying decisions.

articles.title: QS ranks MIT the world’s No. 1 university for 2026-27
articles.description: Ranking at the top for the 15th year in a row, the Institute also places first in 12 subject areas.

articles.title: Birth Control Pill May Increase Risk of Binge Eating — but Self-Monitoring Could Help Reduce the Behavior
articles.description: Learn more about oral contraceptives and the impact they can have on disordered eating, and how self-monitoring may help mitigate the effect.

articles.title: Ancient Plague Killed Hunter-Gatherer Families in Siberia 5,500 Years Ago
articles.description: Learn how DNA from teeth buried near Lake Baikal showed that plague was already deadly before the rise of cities, farming, and efficient flea-borne spread.

articles.title: "Well-Preserved Time Capsule" of an 18th-Century Shipwreck Found Nearly 2,000 Feet Below Norwegian Waters
articles.description: Learn how a Norwegian watchmaker found a merchant ship nearly 2,000 feet below the Skagerrak, its porcelain cargo, and unopened crates still inside.

articles.title: Nuclear waste could be turned into advanced reactor fuel with US firms’ new deal
articles.description: A new partnership between Oklo and Standard Nuclear aims to create a secure, domestic supply chain for advanced reactor fuel.

articles.title: AI for discovery science and healthcare delivery
articles.description: AI's effect on scientific R&D and healthcare delivery and how policy can support further innovation and responsible growth.

articles.title: Asteroid the size of 38 Lubavitcher Rebbes to pass Earth on Chabad head's yahrzeit - NASA
articles.description: Asteroid 2003 LN6, the size of 38 Menachem Mendel Schneersons, is set to pass the Earth on June 18, the anniversary of the death of the Chabad movement leader.

articles.title: In game theory, generalists sometimes win out over specialists
articles.description: Researchers show that for certain kinds of games, an overlooked class of algorithms performs much better than expected.

articles.title: What Would Happen if the Sun Stopped? Part 3: The Photon Traffic Jam
articles.description: A photon born in the Sun's core takes around 100,000 years to fight its way to the surface, bouncing through a random walk so inefficient that the light on your face is older than human civilization. Why the Sun's surface is a hundred-millennia-delayed broadcast.

articles.title: The nose knows: Electric schnoz can smell when your food’s gone bad
articles.description: A new proof-of-concept device created by UC Berkeley researchers can sniff out gases emitted by harmful bacteria, allergens and other food safety hazards. The post The nose knows: Electric schnoz can smell when your food’s gone bad appeared first on Berkeley News .

articles.title: A deadly fungus that can infect cats and people is spreading
articles.description: It’s just a matter of time before Sporothrix brasiliensis reaches the U.S. a CDC expert says.

articles.title: Trump Administration to Pay $765 Million to Cancel 4 More Wind Projects
articles.description: It’s the third such deal the Interior Department has struck to pay firms to abandon plans for offshore turbines.

articles.title: Snap’s Stock Plunges the Moment It Reveals Its Comically Gigantic AR Glasses
articles.description: "Who the hell designed this monstrosity?" The post Snap’s Stock Plunges the Moment It Reveals Its Comically Gigantic AR Glasses appeared first on Futurism .

articles.title: Flexible cryogenic cables solve a challenge in quantum system development
articles.description: A prototype wiring system for dilution refrigerators could advance the realization of practical quantum computers.

articles.title: UB picked as New York's hub for Great Lakes research
articles.description: The University at Buffalo was awarded $5 million to host the Great Lakes Research Consortium.

articles.title: Payload electronics and flight software being developed for NASA's Landsat 10 spacecraft
articles.description: Southwest Research Institute (SwRI) is developing electronics and flight software for the NASA/U.S. Geological Survey (USGS) Landsat 10 satellite, which is set to launch in 2031 to collect new and improved Earth observation data. The mission continues NASA's and USGS's partnership on the ongoing Landsat program, which has launched nine satellites since 1972 and provided more than 10 million images of Earth's surface.

articles.title: EU ministers reach consensus on Biotech Act directive
articles.description: Amid Europe’s push to catch up in the biotechnology field, the EU health ministers agreed how to tweak rules on modified micro-organisms (GMMs) and the processing of organs.

articles.title: The Funniest Possible Thing Happened After Trump Painted the Lincoln Reflecting Pool Blue
articles.description: Nature had other ideas. The post The Funniest Possible Thing Happened After Trump Painted the Lincoln Reflecting Pool Blue appeared first on Futurism .

articles.title: 13 student projects earn CIDA Research Innovation Fund awards
articles.description: Thirteen Cornell students will spend the summer advancing new technologies for agriculture after receiving 2026 Research Innovation Fund awards from the Cornell Institute for Digital Agriculture.

articles.title: Israeli treatment that spared burn surgeries now aims to save limbs
articles.description: An Israeli biotech company that transformed burn care is now testing whether the same pineapple-based technology can help millions of patients suffering from chronic wounds that refuse to heal.

articles.title: 4 nighttime habits to adopt right now to slow down the aging process
articles.description: Experts explain how a few simple changes to your evening routine can contribute to heart, brain, and metabolic health.

articles.title: UK’s new nuclear control room simulator mimics multiple reactor types safely
articles.description: The new, highly reconfigurable nuclear control room simulator uses industrial-grade software to model diverse reactor designs.

articles.title: STAT+: FDA appears open to Moderna’s flu vaccine ahead of adcomm
articles.description: UniQure will submit Huntington's therapy for approval, Eli Lilly buys 4E Therapeutics, and more biotech news

articles.title: 'High-Res' is the Secret to Finding Alien Life with the Next Great Space Telescope
articles.description: We’re still in the definition phase of the Habitable Worlds Observatory (HWO), but it seems like every week a new research group comes out with a paper helping to contribute to what is shaping up to be one of the most important space telescopes of the 2040s. A new paper from a team of researchers led by Daniel Jaffe of the University of Texas at Austin contributes to this ongoing definition work by arguing that it’s time HWO adopted a high-resolution near-IR spectroscopy capability, - which sounds great in practice, but so far hasn’t been attempted due to technological limitations. But, according to the paper, two recent inventions finally make a working version of an extremely high resolution exoplanet hunter viable.

articles.title: Why Botticelli's Birth of Venus has a wonky eye: Scientists claim the model for the world-renowned painting had a brain tumour that caused a squint
articles.description: According to researchers from Queen Mary, University of London, Simonetta Vespucci - the model for the world-renowned painting - had a brain tumour that caused a squint.

articles.title: Why scientists are concerned about the AMOC, and how it could impact the East Coast
articles.description: New research shows that the important AMOC is already slowing down and bringing it closer to a tipping point and eventual shutdown. The impacts would be significant for Atlantic Canada and the planet.

articles.title: Elon Musk Says Dollars Will Be Replaced By 'Mass & Energy,' Reaffirms Lunar Factories, Mass Drivers For AI Goals
articles.description: Space Exploration Technologies Corp. (NASDAQ: SPCX ) and Tesla Inc. (NASDAQ: TSLA ) CEO Elon Musk has reaffirmed his goals of establishing outer space manufacturing capabilities in pursuit of developing deep space AI compute. Elon Musk Touts Lunar Factories, Mass Drivers The trillionaire took to X on Tuesday, sharing how humanity could harness the power of the Sun to fulfill AI goals. In the thread of the post, user James Stephenson asked the SpaceX CEO if the Earth’s orbit would suffice to harness enough solar energy to fulfill the AI goals. data-variant="card" data-news-mode="manual" > Read Also: Elon Musk Revives A 10-Year-Old Promise After SpaceX's Record-Breaking IPO Can you get to 1 millionth of the sun's energy with sun synchronous Earth orbit alone? ... or will you need to launch many satellites into solar orbit outside Earth's gravity well to accomplish that? ... Full story available on Benzinga.com

articles.title: STAT+: A prominent VC explains why she’s against U.S. restrictions on investment in China’s drug industry
articles.description: Should the federal government try to slow — or even block — U.S. biotech deals that could benefit China?

articles.title: New study: Possible link between weight-loss drugs and slowing cancer spread
articles.description: A broad study found a link between the use of GLP–1 receptor agonists and a decreased risk of metastatic progression in four types of cancer.

articles.title: Fire-loving fungi are nature's first responders after wildfire, readying the soil for rebirth
articles.description: Within weeks of a wildfire, an orange crust coats deadwood and the charred forest floor, creating an otherworldly landscape that still seems to be smoking. But instead of continued destruction, it’s a signal of rebirth: tiny fungi are colonizing the wreckage.

articles.title: The US government can shut off access to AI at will. What does this mean for Australia?
articles.description: The risks of depending on US-based AI models are becoming clear.

articles.title: Do pets really help humans cope with stress? New study challenges that
articles.description: Interacting more intensely does not provide more stress relief, researchers say

articles.title: Valerio Therapeutics Appoints New Chief Executive Officer
articles.description: The biotechnology firm has selected Gilles Besin to serve as its new CEO, effective July 1.

articles.title: Botox and fillers – out. The new methods for taut facial skin
articles.description: A longevity and aesthetics expert explains why the beauty world is abandoning the war on wrinkles and moving toward preserving skin health over the years.

articles.title: Lava planet has hydrogen-rich, active atmosphere
articles.description: It’s 2158, and you’re chugging away on your PhD in Planetary Volcanology from the University of Utopia Planitia on Mars. Graduate students still get paid a sub-living wage, so you’ve been stuck eating freeze-dried ramen for the past three years. You’ve completed studying Jupiter’s moon, Io, but now you have to leave the solar system for a good exoplanet analog. While Io’s volcanism is caused by tidal heating, you need an exoplanet whose volcanism is caused by extreme heat from its host star. You recently secured funding from the Exoplanet Research Institute for a faster-than-light (FTL) ship, but the exoplanet is required to be less than 50 light-years away.

articles.title: Saskatchewan health technology leaders looking to province to adopt innovations
articles.description: Leaders in Saskatchewan's healthcare technology sector say they have answers to the province's most pressing health care concerns, but need the province to sign on to deliver them.

articles.title: 1,650 radio dishes planned for Nevada to comprise ‘World’s Most Sensitive Radio Telescope’
articles.description: Hundreds of radio dishes planned for a remote Nevada desert will scan millions of stars, galaxies, and other mysterious or unknown activities like black holes, pulsars and radio bursts.

articles.title: Chess emerging as tool for innovation on brain-computer interface systems
articles.description: Chess and brain-computer interface research came together at a clinical symposium held at the Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen, with FIDE Treasurer Zhu Chen among the invited guests. The event followed recent demonstrations of BCI technology through online chess, including Neuralink patient Noland Arbaugh's game against Anna Cramling. Researchers discussed medical applications for neurological conditions, while chess was presented as a useful tool for exploring cognition, digital interaction and future clinical testing. | Photo: Liang Ziming

articles.title: Seoul hospital to promote biotech startups at BIO 2026
articles.description: Seoul National University Hospital will use its first independent BIO convention booth to help five S. Korean biotech startups find global partners.

articles.title: New atom-level calculations show transistors could shrink below 4 nanometers
articles.description: KAIST researchers predict how small future transistors can become before quantum effects take over.

articles.title: Baby fossils reveal link between human and Neanderthal development
articles.description: A UQ skeletal histologist has led analysis of baby teeth and bone fossils from 50,000-70,0000 years ago that reveal the growth of Neanderthals was remarkably similar to modern humans.
